	"rendered_dump_text": "========================================\nFASTCHAT V2 PROMPT SNAPSHOT\nStream run: c0fce4d8-bf29-488a-a0f8-32e373144c77\nSession:    71a1bd2b-06b7-4bb4-984a-edb24deb24ae\nContext:    project_create\nEntity ID:  none\nProject ID: none\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\nTools (4): skill_load, tool_search, tool_schema, create_onto_project\nMessage count: 2\nSystem prompt length: 12805 chars (~3202 tokens)\n========================================\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nSYSTEM PROMPT\n────────────────────────────────────────\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent focus:\n- The user is trying to create a new BuildOS project right now.\n- No existing project or focus entity exists yet; treat the user message as the source of truth for the initial project.\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project creation assistant that can turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure.\n\nProject creation workflow:\n- Turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure with a clear name, type_key, description / props, and only the entities and relationships the user actually described.\n- project.type_key must start with \"project.\", for example project.creative.novel.\n- Always include entities: [] and relationships: [] arrays even when empty.\n- If the user stated an outcome, add one goal. If they listed concrete actions, add only those task entities. Add plans or milestones only when they clearly described workstreams, phases, or date-driven structure.\n- Entity labels: goal / plan / metric use `name`; task / milestone / document / risk use `title`; requirement uses `text`; source uses `uri`. Milestones also require `due_at`.\n- For goal entities, use dedicated fields like target_date and measurement_criteria instead of burying them only in props. If the user gives a month/day without a year, infer the next plausible future date in the user's locale.\n- **Connect the graph.** When the user has both a goal and tasks, emit containment relationships linking every task (child) to that goal (parent). A project with 1 goal + N tasks should produce N+ goal-task containment edges; leaving tasks unlinked defeats the graph model.\n- Relationship item shape: every entry must reference entities by `{ temp_id, kind }`. Use the form `{ from: { temp_id, kind }, to: { temp_id, kind } }` (or the array form `[{ temp_id, kind }, { temp_id, kind }]`) with an explicit `type` such as `contains`. Never pass raw temp_id strings like `[\"g1\", \"t1\"]`.\n- Use clarifications[] only when critical information cannot be reasonably inferred; still send the project skeleton.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when a required detail blocks a safe create payload.\n- After creation succeeds, continue inside the created project instead of staying in abstract creation mode.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nProject creation scope:\n- This chat is in project_create mode before a project exists.\n- No existing project graph or focus entity is preloaded by default.\n- Use the user message as the source of truth for the new project; fetch schema guidance only if a create field is uncertain.\n\n## Project Creation Boundaries\n\nCreation boundaries:\n- Use the user idea and project_create mode state as the working context.\n- Do not load existing workspace/project data just to begin project creation.\n- Prefer the project_creation skill for multi-step creation, then call the direct create tool once the payload is ready.\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nLITE SECTION BREAKDOWN\n────────────────────────────────────────\n1. identity_mission - Identity and Mission Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent focus:\n- The user is trying to create a new BuildOS project right now.\n- No existing project or focus entity exists yet; treat the user message as the source of truth for the initial project.\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project creation assistant that can turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure.\n\nProject creation workflow:\n- Turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure with a clear name, type_key, description / props, and only the entities and relationships the user actually described.\n- project.type_key must start with \"project.\", for example project.creative.novel.\n- Always include entities: [] and relationships: [] arrays even when empty.\n- If the user stated an outcome, add one goal. If they listed concrete actions, add only those task entities. Add plans or milestones only when they clearly described workstreams, phases, or date-driven structure.\n- Entity labels: goal / plan / metric use `name`; task / milestone / document / risk use `title`; requirement uses `text`; source uses `uri`. Milestones also require `due_at`.\n- For goal entities, use dedicated fields like target_date and measurement_criteria instead of burying them only in props. If the user gives a month/day without a year, infer the next plausible future date in the user's locale.\n- **Connect the graph.** When the user has both a goal and tasks, emit containment relationships linking every task (child) to that goal (parent). A project with 1 goal + N tasks should produce N+ goal-task containment edges; leaving tasks unlinked defeats the graph model.\n- Relationship item shape: every entry must reference entities by `{ temp_id, kind }`. Use the form `{ from: { temp_id, kind }, to: { temp_id, kind } }` (or the array form `[{ temp_id, kind }, { temp_id, kind }]`) with an explicit `type` such as `contains`. Never pass raw temp_id strings like `[\"g1\", \"t1\"]`.\n- Use clarifications[] only when critical information cannot be reasonably inferred; still send the project skeleton.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when a required detail blocks a safe create payload.\n- After creation succeeds, continue inside the created project instead of staying in abstract creation mode.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nProject creation scope:\n- This chat is in project_create mode before a project exists.\n- No existing project graph or focus entity is preloaded by default.\n- Use the user message as the source of truth for the new project; fetch schema guidance only if a create field is uncertain.\n\n## Project Creation Boundaries\n\nCreation boundaries:\n- Use the user idea and project_create mode state as the working context.\n- Do not load existing workspace/project data just to begin project creation.\n- Prefer the project_creation skill for multi-step creation, then call the direct create tool once the payload is ready.\n\n[2] role=user\nI'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n",
	"system_prompt_sha256": "b11179936b902ecdf05e2c8bde5494f4634e72e109c656f8cf4dc8d8dccccbec",
	"messages_sha256": "a346ffc7da1861f5b84cf94661122e6fdad7d4a3f4d649cdb97a5e80a9c953b6",

	"message_chars": 13350,
	"approx_prompt_tokens": 3338,
	"created_at": "2026-04-17T21:26:10.396519+00:00"
}

#### Request Message

```text
Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.

Issues to address:

- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne
- The pacing in the middle feels slow
- Add more sensory details about the forge

Chapter 3 plans:

- Elena's first attempt at magical forging
- Introduce the Shadow King's herald
- Foreshadow the prophecy

Also need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2.
```

{
	"id": "6fb497c0-a635-48a0-a88d-3ba54bed3c33",

	"system_prompt": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- get_onto_document_details\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document\n- get_document_tree\n- move_document_in_tree\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. Stay in project.\n- For audits, health reviews, stress tests, blockers, stale work, or gap analysis -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_audit' }) before the analysis if the answer is multi-step or evidence-heavy.\n- For forecasts, schedule risk, slippage, scenarios, or \"are we on track\" -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_forecast' }) before the analysis if the answer depends on assumptions or multiple signals.\n- Use the current project_id and project-focused direct tools; do not invent project_audit or project_forecast sessions.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- inside The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a)\n- The bounded index below is for orientation and exact IDs only; it is not the full cache.\n- Fetch full entity details before non-obvious Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- get_onto_document_details\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document\n- get_document_tree\n- move_document_in_tree\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. Stay in project.\n- For audits, health reviews, stress tests, blockers, stale work, or gap analysis -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_audit' }) before the analysis if the answer is multi-step or evidence-heavy.\n- For forecasts, schedule risk, slippage, scenarios, or \"are we on track\" -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_forecast' }) before the analysis if the answer depends on assumptions or multiple signals.\n- Use the current project_id and project-focused direct tools; do not invent project_audit or project_forecast sessions.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- 			"content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
		},

			"role": "assistant",
			"content": "It has 1 goal, 7 tasks, 1 document, 9 edges.\n\nContext has shifted to this project.\n\nUser-facing response rules:\n\n- Name each successful write by title or what changed.\n\n- Direct prose, no planning or instructions echoed.\n\n- After creation, continue inside the created project.\n\n- Keep conversation useful for next step.\n\nMy previous message was the tool call.\n\nNow, craft the final user-facing response.\n\nStructure a helpful summary:\n\n- Confirm creation with ID.\n\n- List the goal and tasks.\n\n- Note the connections.\n\n- Offer next steps, since now focused on the project.\n\nIn the tool result: counts include \"documents\":1, which is likely the context_document I included.\n\nIn tool call: I had project description, but no explicit context_document.\n\nResult has documents:1, maybe from that.\n\nAnyway, ground in results.\n\nBut in tool call it was correct: \"(The Shadow King)\".\n\nIn my simulated response, I typed wrong, but fix it.\n\nFinal response should be direct, natural prose.\n\nEnd by prompting next action.I've created your new project **\"The Last Ember\"** (ID: 940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a), a `project.creative.novel` in **planning** state starting October 1, 2024.\n\nIt includes:\n\n- **Goal**: Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\n\n- **7 tasks** contained within the goal:\n\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n\n- Outline first three chapters\n\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n\nThis sets up 9 edges linking everything properly, plus a context document with the main plot summary.\n\nWe're now working inside this project—what's next?\n\nTackle research for blacksmithing, outline a task, add milestones, or something else?"

							"description": "Optional top-level tool family filter. Use this to narrow the search space once you know the tool family."

							"type": "integer",
							"description": "Maximum number of matches to return."
						},
						"query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Natural-language description of the operation you need, not project/task content. Good examples: \"workspace overview\", \"update existing task state\", or \"move document in tree\". Bad example: \"chapter 3 plans\" when you are trying to find project data."
						},
						"entity": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional entity filter such as \"task\", \"project\", or \"document\". For example: entity=\"task\" with kind=\"write\" for task mutations."
						},
						"capability": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional BuildOS capability id or path such as \"overview\", \"project_creation\", or \"capabilities.calendar\". Prefer this when the capability family is already clear."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Discover candidate BuildOS tools on demand. Use this only when the exact op is still unknown after context and skill guidance. Search for the operation you need, not workspace data."

				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["target"],
					"properties": {
						"reason": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Brief user-visible reason for the context change, grounded in the latest request."
						},
						"target": {
							"enum": ["global", "project"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Target context. Use global for workspace/all-projects zoom-out. Use project for durable focus on one project."

						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Exact project UUID when zooming into a known project."

						"project_query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project name or phrase to resolve when zooming into a project and project_id is unknown."
						}
					}

				"description": "Change the durable chat context when the latest user request should strategically zoom into one project or zoom out to the workspace.\n\nUse this early in the turn before answering when:\n- The user explicitly asks to zoom, switch, focus, go to a project, or go back to all projects.\n- The current context is global/workspace and the latest request is primarily about one identifiable project, so project tools should be loaded for the rest of the turn.\n- The current context is one project and the latest request is clearly about a different project, and the user appears to be moving focus rather than doing a brief comparison.\n- The user asks about all projects, workspace status, cross-project priorities, or wants to zoom out.\n\nDo not use this for ambiguous project names, one-off comparisons across multiple projects, or brief mentions that can be answered from current context. For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."

							"default": 10,
							"maximum": 25,
							"description": "Maximum number of results (capped at 25)"
						},
						"query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Search text to match inside the specified project (required)"
						},
						"types": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"enum": [
									"project",
									"task",
									"goal",
									"plan",
									"milestone",
									"document",
									"risk",
									"requirement",
									"image"
								],
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional entity type filters for narrowing results"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project ID to scope the search to (required)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Primary agent search inside one BuildOS project. Use this when a project_id is known or the chat is already scoped to a project.\nReturns typed ontology matches with snippets so you can quickly shortlist items before loading details."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "list_onto_tasks",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"limit": {
							"type": "number",
							"default": 20,
							"maximum": 50,
							"description": "Maximum tasks to return"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter by state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter tasks by project ID"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "List tasks from the ontology system (onto_tasks table). Returns abbreviated task information including id, title, state, and type.\nUse this for queries about ontology tasks, plans with tasks, or project task lists."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "list_onto_documents",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"limit": {
							"type": "number",
							"default": 20,
							"maximum": 50,
							"description": "Maximum number of documents to return"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter by document type key (e.g., document.context.project, document.knowledge.research)"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter by document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter documents by project ID"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "List documents from the ontology system (onto_documents table). Returns document summaries plus markdown heading outlines (H1/H2/H3), not full body content.\nUse get_onto_document_details when full document content is needed."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_task",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional JSON properties"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "ISO due date"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional primary containment parent"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional goal UUID"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"is_primary": {
										"type": "boolean"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional containment parents"
						},
						"plan_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional plan UUID"
						},
						"priority": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
						},
						"start_at": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "ISO start date"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "task.execute",
							"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "todo",
							"description": "Initial state. Valid: todo, in_progress, blocked, done"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID"
						},
						"connections": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"rel": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"intent": {
										"enum": ["containment", "semantic"],
										"type": "string"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional semantic or containment links"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member handles, e.g. @dj."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member actor UUIDs. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID"
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_task",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["task_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Properties to merge with existing props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New due date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional goal UUID that this task supports (null clears)"
						},
						"task_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task UUID (required)"
						},
						"priority": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "New priority (1-5)"
						},
						"start_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New start date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Work mode taxonomy: task.{work_mode}[.{specialization}]. Modes: execute, create, refine, research, review, coordinate, admin, plan."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional project UUID used for assignee handle resolution. If omitted, the task project is resolved automatically."
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional assignee handles like [\"@jim\"]. Resolved against active project members by name/email local-part."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional full replacement assignee actor ID list (UUIDs) for active project members only. Prefer assignee_handles unless IDs were just retrieved from project members. Use [] to clear assignees. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID this task targets (null clears)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Update an existing task in the ontology system.\nCan modify title, description, state, priority, assignees, scheduling, and custom properties.\nOnly updates fields that are provided - omitted fields remain unchanged."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "get_onto_document_details",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document ID to retrieve"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Get complete details for a specific ontology document including content, description, and metadata.\nUse when you need the full document before editing or linking it."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title", "description"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional properties/metadata"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document title (required)"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"is_primary": {
										"type": "boolean"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
						},
						"position": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "document.default",
							"description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
						},
						"parent_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "draft",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID (required)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document title"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document type key"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document"
						},
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document UUID (required)"
						},
						"update_strategy": {
							"enum": ["replace", "append", "merge_llm"],
							"type": "string",
							"default": "replace",
							"description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
						},
						"merge_instructions": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "get_document_tree",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id"],
					"properties": {
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project ID to get the document tree for (required)"
						},
						"include_content": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"default": false,
							"description": "Include full document content bodies. Only applies when include_documents is true."
						},
						"include_documents": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"default": false,
							"description": "Include document metadata and unlinked document list. Set true when you need titles or orphaned docs."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Get the hierarchical document tree structure for a project.\nReturns the tree structure; set include_documents to include document metadata and unlinked docs.\nDocuments are organized in a wiki-like tree structure with folders (documents that have children) and leaf documents."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "move_document_in_tree",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID (required)"
						},
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document UUID to move (required)"
						},
						"new_position": {
							"type": "number",
							"default": 0,
							"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at top."
						},
						"new_parent_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New parent document ID (null or omitted places the document at root level)."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Move or insert an existing document within the project's doc_structure.\nUse this to nest existing or unlinked documents under a parent or reorder siblings.\nIf new_parent_id is null or omitted, the document is placed at root level.\nAlways pass the exact document_id from get_document_tree/list_onto_documents (do not pass document titles)."
			}
		}
	],
	"request_payload": {
		"entity_id": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
		"session_id": "71a1bd2b-06b7-4bb4-984a-edb24deb24ae",
		"context_type": "project",
		"project_focus": {
			"focusType": "project-wide",
			"projectId": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
			"projectName": "The Last Ember",
			"focusEntityId": null,
			"focusEntityName": null
		},
		"stream_run_id": "042f7969-3bb7-44ce-95bb-425f29c92b37",
		"client_turn_id": "cb1c018a-d9c9-45e3-945d-6f071332fa27",
		"prompt_variant": "lite_seed_v1",
		"voice_note_group_id": null
	},
	"prompt_sections": {
		"data_keys": [
			"context_meta",
			"doc_structure",
			"documents",
			"events",
			"events_window",
			"goals",
			"members",
			"milestones",
			"plans",
			"project",
			"project_intelligence",
			"tasks"
		],
		"entity_id": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
		"project_id": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
		"context_type": "project",
		"project_name": "The Last Ember",
		"lite_sections": [
			{
				"id": "identity_mission",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 602,
				"title": "Identity and Mission",
				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
				"estimatedTokens": 151
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 3996,
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.static_capability_skill_catalog",
				"content": "Think in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.",
				"estimatedTokens": 999
			},
			{
				"id": "tool_surface_dynamic",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 367,
				"slots": {
					"totalTools": 16,
					"contextType": "project",
					"directTools": [
						"change_chat_context",
						"get_project_overview",
						"get_onto_project_details",
						"search_project",
						"list_onto_tasks",
						"list_onto_documents",
						"create_onto_task",
						"update_onto_task",
						"get_onto_document_details",
						"create_onto_document",
						"update_onto_document",
						"get_document_tree",
						"move_document_in_tree"
					],
					"discoveryTools": ["skill_load", "tool_search", "tool_schema"]
				},
				"title": "Current Tool Surface",
				"source": "lite.context_tool_surface",
				"content": "Discovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- get_onto_document_details\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document\n- get_document_tree\n- move_document_in_tree",
				"estimatedTokens": 92
			},
			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 1680,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.",
				"estimatedTokens": 420
			},
			{
				"id": "safety_data_rules",
				"kind": "mixed",
				"chars": 3324,
				"slots": {
					"memberRoleBulletRendered": false
				},
				"title": "Safety and Data Rules",
				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",
				"estimatedTokens": 831
			},
