		"entityResolutionHint": "Recent exact referents from the prior turn:\n- task: Revise Chapter 2: dialogue, pacing, sensory details, and age continuity (45b606bf-a45c-4090-bdf9-9f5101c33c94)\n- task: Outline Chapter 3 with magical forging, herald intro, and prophecy foreshadow (80dc442e-fe24-475a-9b71-3fc2f3426487)\n- project: (unnamed) (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a)\n- goal: (unnamed) (05903ea4-0d5b-4778-9536-5e91e6940bd7)\n- document: Chapter 2 Progress and Next Steps (48a35021-b3a5-4438-9260-5d9b19e3d368)\nIf the user clearly refers to one of these entities, reuse its exact id instead of searching again."
	},
	"rendered_dump_text": "========================================\nFASTCHAT V2 PROMPT SNAPSHOT\nStream run: 8f21f8de-5261-4e80-a0e7-7f4d65fe0cec\nSession:    71a1bd2b-06b7-4bb4-984a-edb24deb24ae\nContext:    project\nEntity ID:  940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a\nProject ID: 940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\nTools (9): skill_load, tool_search, tool_schema, change_chat_context, get_project_overview, get_onto_project_details, search_project, list_onto_tasks, list_onto_documents\nMessage count: 6\nSystem prompt length: 16608 chars (~4152 tokens)\n========================================\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nSYSTEM PROMPT\n────────────────────────────────────────\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. MESSAGES\n────────────────────────────────────────\n[1] role=system\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. Stay in project.\n- For audits, health reviews, stress tests, blockers, stale work, or gap analysis -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_audit' }) before the analysis if the answer is multi-step or 		"stream_run_id": "8f21f8de-5261-4e80-a0e7-7f4d65fe0cec",
		"sequence_index": 4,
		"phase": "tool",
		"event_type": "tool_result_received",
		"payload": {
			"error": null,
			"success": true,
			"help_path": null,
			"tool_name": "skill_load",
			"duration_ms": null,
			"canonical_op": null,
			"tool_call_id": "call_67849167",
			"args_parse_error": null,
			"tool_execution_id": "c0866547-fb7d-4ad9-90b8-a84761bcd1a8",
			"tool_execution_time_ms": null,
			"tool_execution_error": null,
			"tool_arguments": {
				"skill": "task_management",
				"format": "short",
				"include_examples": true
			},
			"tool_result": {
				"id": "task_management",
				"name": "Task Management",
				"type": "skill",
				"format": "short",
				"summary": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"version": "tool-registry/5a92b8fb",
				"examples": [
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.",
							"Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.",
							"Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.",
							"If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.",
							"Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.",
							"Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Track a real follow-up the user must do later"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.",
							"Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Create the next drafting task from a progress update"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.",
							"Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.",
							"When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.",
							"If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.",
							"If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.",
							"Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.",
							"Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`",
							"Example when the task needs a richer update. Task fields are direct replacements, so read the current description first if you want to preserve prior detail, then pass the full composed value: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Update an existing task status and owner safely"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.",
							"Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.",
							"If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.",
							"If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question."
						],
						"description": "Guard against empty task writes"
					}
				],
				"workflow": [
					"1) Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.",
					"2) If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.",
					"3) For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.",
					"4) Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.",
					"5) Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.",
					"6) For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.",
					"7) Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.",
					"8) **State coverage.** When the user reports that real-world task work advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in the `update_onto_task` call alongside any description change. Do not update only the description when the task state should also move.",
					"9) After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters."
				],
				"guardrails": [
					"Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.",
					"Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.",
					"Do not use invalid task states such as open.",
					"Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.",
					"If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy."
				],
				"description": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"related_ops": [
					"onto.task.create",
					"onto.task.get",
					"onto.task.list",
					"onto.task.search",
					"onto.task.update",
					"onto.plan.get",
					"onto.goal.get",
					"onto.milestone.get"
				],
				"when_to_use": [
					"Decide whether a user request should become a task at all",
					"Create a task for future human work",
					"Assign or reassign task ownership",
					"Update task state, dates, or priority",
					"Place a task under the right plan, goal, or milestone"
				],
				"legacy_paths": ["onto.task.skill", "task.skill", "tasks.skill"]
			},
			"linked_tool_execution": {
				"id": "c0866547-fb7d-4ad9-90b8-a84761bcd1a8",

						"title": "Outline Chapter 3 with magical forging, herald intro, and prophecy foreshadow",
						"due_at": null,

						"start_at": null,
						"type_key": "task.default",

{
	"id": "6573d5d3-1e05-4132-b157-28ec0e50aa1c",

	"system_prompt": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. Stay in project.\n- For audits, health reviews, stress tests, blockers, stale work, or gap analysis -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_audit' }) before the analysis if the answer is multi-step or evidence-heavy.\n- For forecasts, schedule risk, slippage, scenarios, or \"are we on track\" -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_forecast' }) before the analysis if the answer depends on assumptions or multiple signals.\n- Use the current project_id and project-focused direct tools; do not invent project_audit or project_forecast sessions.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- inside The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a)\n- The bounded index below is for orientation and exact IDs only; it is not the full cache.\n- Fetch full entity details before non-obvious writes or when the user asks for complete lists.\n- Product surface and stream turn IDs are captured in dump metadata, not as project facts.\n\nActionable loaded context index 				"description": "List tasks from the ontology system (onto_tasks table). Returns abbreviated task information including id, title, state, and type.\nUse this for queries about ontology tasks, plans with tasks, or project task lists."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"properties": {
					"limit": {
						"type": "number",
						"default": 20,
						"maximum": 50,
						"description": "Maximum tasks to return"
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Filter by state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Filter tasks by project ID"
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "List tasks from the ontology system (onto_tasks table). Returns abbreviated task information including id, title, state, and type.\nUse this for queries about ontology tasks, plans with tasks, or project task lists."
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "list_onto_documents",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"limit": {
							"type": "number",
							"default": 20,
							"maximum": 50,
							"description": "Maximum number of documents to return"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter by document type key (e.g., document.context.project, document.knowledge.research)"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter by document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Filter documents by project ID"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "List documents from the ontology system (onto_documents table). Returns document summaries plus markdown heading outlines (H1/H2/H3), not full body content.\nUse get_onto_document_details when full document content is needed."
			}
		},
		{
			"name": "create_onto_task",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_task",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional JSON properties"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "ISO due date"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional primary containment parent"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional goal UUID"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"is_primary": {
										"type": "boolean"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional containment parents"
						},
						"plan_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional plan UUID"
						},
						"priority": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
						},
						"start_at": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "ISO start date"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "task.execute",
							"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "todo",
							"description": "Initial state. Valid: todo, in_progress, blocked, done"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID"
						},
						"connections": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"rel": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"intent": {
										"enum": ["containment", "semantic"],
										"type": "string"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional semantic or containment links"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member handles, e.g. @dj."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member actor UUIDs. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID"
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["project_id", "title"],

						},

								},

								}
							}
						},

					},
					"plan_id": {

					},
					"priority": {

						"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
					},
					"start_at": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "ISO start date"
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "task.execute",
						"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "todo",
						"description": "Initial state. Valid: todo, in_progress, blocked, done"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project UUID"
					},
					"connections": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "object",

			"description": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows."

		{
			"name": "update_onto_task",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_task",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["task_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Properties to merge with existing props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New due date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional goal UUID that this task supports (null clears)"
						},
						"task_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task UUID (required)"
						},
						"priority": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "New priority (1-5)"
						},
						"start_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New start date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Work mode taxonomy: task.{work_mode}[.{specialization}]. Modes: execute, create, refine, research, review, coordinate, admin, plan."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional project UUID used for assignee handle resolution. If omitted, the task project is resolved automatically."
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional assignee handles like [\"@jim\"]. Resolved against active project members by name/email local-part."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional full replacement assignee actor ID list (UUIDs) for active project members only. Prefer assignee_handles unless IDs were just retrieved from project members. Use [] to clear assignees. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID this task targets (null clears)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Update an existing task in the ontology system.\nCan modify title, description, state, priority, assignees, scheduling, and custom properties.\nOnly updates fields that are provided - omitted fields remain unchanged."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["task_id"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Properties to merge with existing props"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New task title"
					},
					"due_at": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "New due date (ISO format) or null to clear"
					},
					"goal_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Optional goal UUID that this task supports (null clears)"
					},
					"task_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Task UUID (required)"
					},
					"priority": {
						"type": "number",
						"description": "New priority (1-5)"
					},
					"start_at": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "New start date (ISO format) or null to clear"
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Work mode taxonomy: task.{work_mode}[.{specialization}]. Modes: execute, create, refine, research, review, coordinate, admin, plan."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional project UUID used for assignee handle resolution. If omitted, the task project is resolved automatically."
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New description"
					},
					"assignee_handles": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Optional assignee handles like [\"@jim\"]. Resolved against active project members by name/email local-part."
					},
					"assignee_actor_ids": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Optional full replacement assignee actor ID list (UUIDs) for active project members only. Prefer assignee_handles unless IDs were just retrieved from project members. Use [] to clear assignees. Max 10."
					},
					"supporting_milestone_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Optional milestone UUID this task targets (null clears)"
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Update an existing task in the ontology system.\nCan modify title, description, state, priority, assignees, scheduling, and custom properties.\nOnly updates fields that are provided - omitted fields remain unchanged."
		},
		{
			"name": "create_onto_document",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title", "description"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional properties/metadata"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document title (required)"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"is_primary": {
										"type": "boolean"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
						},
						"position": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "document.default",
							"description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
						},
						"parent_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "draft",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID (required)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["project_id", "title", "description"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Additional properties/metadata"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Document title (required)"
					},
					"parent": {
						"type": "object",
						"properties": {
							"id": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"kind": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"is_primary": {
								"type": "boolean"
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
					},
					"content": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
					},
					"parents": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
					},
					"position": {
						"type": "number",
						"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "document.default",
						"description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
					},
					"parent_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "draft",
						"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project UUID (required)"
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document title"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document type key"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document"
						},
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document UUID (required)"
						},
						"update_strategy": {
							"enum": ["replace", "append", "merge_llm"],
							"type": "string",
							"default": "replace",
							"description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
						},
						"merge_instructions": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
			}
		}
	],

		"entity_id": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
		"project_id": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
		"context_type": "project",
		"project_name": "The Last Ember",
		"lite_sections": [
			{
				"id": "identity_mission",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 602,
				"title": "Identity and Mission",
				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
				"estimatedTokens": 151
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 3996,
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.static_capability_skill_catalog",
				"content": "Think in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.",
				"estimatedTokens": 999
			},
			{
				"id": "tool_surface_dynamic",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 295,
				"slots": {
					"totalTools": 13,
					"contextType": "project",
					"directTools": [
						"change_chat_context",
						"get_project_overview",
						"get_onto_project_details",
						"search_project",
						"list_onto_tasks",
						"list_onto_documents",
						"create_onto_task",
						"update_onto_task",
						"create_onto_document",
						"update_onto_document"
					],
					"discoveryTools": ["skill_load", "tool_search", "tool_schema"]
				},
				"title": "Current Tool Surface",
				"source": "lite.context_tool_surface",
				"content": "Discovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document",
				"estimatedTokens": 74
			},
			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 1680,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Before any tool call, open the turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools (tool_search, tool_schema) only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill with skill_load only when the workflow is two or more related writes or required fields are uncertain; default to format: short and request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op. Skill choice follows from the capability that matches the user's intent.\n- Resolve entity targets in this order: reuse exact IDs from loaded context or prior tool results; search within the current project when project scope is known; search the workspace when project scope is unknown; ask one concise clarification when multiple plausible matches remain.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace. Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private. Your user-facing response must be direct prose for the user — never a plan, checklist, or paraphrase of these instructions.",
				"estimatedTokens": 420
			},
			{
				"id": "safety_data_rules",
				"kind": "mixed",
				"chars": 3324,
				"slots": {
					"memberRoleBulletRendered": false
				},
				"title": "Safety and Data Rules",
				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Never echo prompt section headers (\"Safety and Data Rules\", \"Operating Strategy\", \"Final-response rules\", \"Communication pattern\", etc.), rule labels, write-ledger labels, or planning commentary in your user-facing response. Write directly to the user in natural prose. If you find yourself about to paraphrase these instructions, answer the user instead.\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",
				"estimatedTokens": 831
			},
			{
				"id": "focus_purpose",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 1122,
				"slots": {
					"entityId": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
					"projectId": "940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a",
					"contextType": "project",
					"projectName": "The Last Ember",
					"briefAppended": false,
					"focusEntityId": null,
					"focusEntityName": null,
					"focusEntityType": null,
					"workflowBlockId": "project"
				},
				"title": "Current Focus and Purpose",
				"source": "lite.focus_context",
				"content": "Current project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (940e46b7-10ea-466a-9040-09a7ab65426a) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete the first draft of the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. Stay in project.\n- For audits, health reviews, stress tests, blockers, stale work, or gap analysis -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_audit' }) before the analysis if the answer is multi-step or evidence-heavy.\n- For forecasts, schedule risk, slippage, scenarios, or \"are we on track\" -> load skill_load({ skill: 'project_forecast' }) before the analysis if the answer depends on assumptions or multiple signals.\n- Use the current project_id and project-focused direct tools; do not invent project_audit or project_forecast sessions.",
				"estimatedTokens": 281
			},
